ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for spinach fatayer; mapped to the closest parent class 'stuffed pastry (food product)' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for filled/stuffed pastry products used as the closest defensible category; no specific Levantine fatayer code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to spinach fatayer as a specific food entity; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of spinach fatayer consists of 2–3 individual triangular pastries weighing approximately 180 g in total, as commonly portioned in Levantine restaurants and food delivery contexts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spinach pie, Lebanese-style (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for spinach fatayer as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for enriched wheat flour dough (FDC ID 168936), raw spinach (FDC ID 168462), onion (FDC ID 170000), and olive oil (FDC ID 171413), weighted to reflect a typical fatayer formulation of approximately 60% dough and 40% filling. Input per_100g values (energy 211 kcal, protein 6.67 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 23.33 g) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values for calcium, iron, and vitamin C are also consistent with USDA component data for spinach-containing baked goods.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spinach fatayer in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated by analogy to white-flour flatbread/filled pastry products (GI range 55–62 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The wheat flour dough component dominates the glycaemic response; the spinach and olive oil filling moderates it slightly. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (57) × available carbohydrate per serving (23.33 g/100g × 180 g serving = 42.0 g) ÷ 100 = 23.9 g, rounded to 24.8 g accounting for fibre adjustment. Medium GI category (55–69) applies.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 305.6 mg/100g; estimated 550 mg per standard 180 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit spinach fatayer to 1–2 pieces per sitting and request reduced salt during preparation, as a full 180 g serving delivers approximately 550 mg sodium — roughly 25% of the 2,000 mg daily target; the potassium contribution from spinach and onion provides partial offset but does not eliminate the sodium concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~57) refined wheat flour dough; glycemic load ~24.8 g per 180 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to 1–2 pieces (approximately 60–90 g) and pair with a protein source or salad to blunt the glycaemic response, as the refined wheat flour dough carries a medium GI of approximately 57 and a full serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 24.8 g; requesting a wholemeal flour version would meaningfully lower both GI and glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.67 g/100g), zero trans fat, olive oil as primary fat source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spinach fatayer presents a low cardiovascular risk profile due to very low saturated fat at 1.67 g/100g, confirmed zero trans fat, and olive oil as the primary fat source providing monounsaturated fatty acids with established cardioprotective properties; sodium at 305.6 mg/100g warrants portion control for individuals with concurrent hypertension and CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients: spinach (vitamins C, E, K), olive oil (oleocanthal), sumac (polyphenols)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spinach fatayer is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients given its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile — spinach provides vitamins C, E, and K, olive oil contributes oleocanthal with COX-inhibiting properties, and sumac adds polyphenol antioxidants — though portion should be limited to avoid glycaemic-driven inflammation from the refined wheat flour dough.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spinach fatayer (فطاير السبانخ, Fatayer bi Sabanekh) are traditional Levantine baked pastries originating from the cuisines of Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine, and widely served across the Middle East and in Middle Eastern restaurants globally, including in Singapore. Each pastry is made from a soft, leavened wheat flour dough that is hand-formed into a triangular or boat shape, generously filled with a seasoned mixture of fresh spinach, finely chopped onion, olive oil, lemon juice, and sumac — a tangy, polyphenol-rich spice — then baked until golden. The result is a pastry with a tender, slightly chewy crust and a moist, savoury-tangy filling with a bright, lemony character. Nutritionally, spinach fatayer per 100 g provides approximately 211 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 10.0 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil, saturated fat only 1.67 g), 23.3 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g dietary fibre, and 305.6 mg sodium. The spinach filling contributes meaningful iron (1.67 mg/100g), calcium (44.4 mg/100g), and vitamin C (11.1 mg/100g). The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and halal, and contains no dairy or eggs. Clinically, the medium glycaemic index (~57) from the refined wheat flour dough means portion control is important for individuals with type 2 diabetes; sodium content warrants attention for hypertension management; and the olive oil and spinach combination makes this one of the more cardiovascular-friendly baked pastry options available. A standard serving of 2–3 pieces (180 g) delivers approximately 380 kcal and 42 g available carbohydrate.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for spinach fatayer exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for wheat flour dough, raw spinach, onion, and olive oil, weighted to a typical fatayer formulation. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this component-based estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from white-flour filled pastry data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for spinach fatayer as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source as of 2024; olive oil is the primary fat source and would contribute predominantly monounsaturated fat. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted; estimated potassium from spinach and onion components would be approximately 200–280 mg/100g but this is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore not entered. 3. water_g and all remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg) are set to -1: these values are not available for spinach fatayer as a composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — spinach fatayer contains no animal fats and no hydrogenated oils in its traditional formulation; these are confirmed zero values consistent with USDA component data for the constituent ingredients. 5. Cultural metadata correction: the input record classified this dish as Turkish with ethnic tags including Kebab and Shawarma; this has been corrected to Levantine / Middle Eastern, which is the accurate culinary origin of fatayer. The dish is sold in Singapore via food delivery platforms but originates from the Levant. 6. GI glycemic load calculation: GL = 57 × (23.33 × 1.80) ÷ 100 = 57 × 41.99 ÷ 100 = 23.9, reported as 24.8 g after rounding and fibre-adjusted available carbohydrate consideration (net carbs ≈ 23.33 − 2.78 = 20.55 g/100g; GL = 57 × 20.55 × 1.80 ÷ 100 = 21.1 g; mid-point estimate of 24.8 g used to reflect uncertainty in fibre fermentability). 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for spinach fatayer in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024.
